citations.description: The fix routes window-absent bindings (no absent_404_count) to absent_in_window_unprobed rather than dangling — a wholesale reclassification, not per-item triage.

Ticket comment from Joe Oakhart: 'Triage result: NO actionable drift to remediate; the 176 are working-as-designed.' This is an attestation on an untagged (codebase-verifiable) criterion and cannot on its own satisfy it.

findings.criterion: Each reported drift is triaged (would_terminal → local archive/delete to propagate; dangling → confirmed 404 GC; unbound_jira → adopt or archive the Jira-native issue; local_gone → investigate).
findings.detail: This is an untagged (codebase-verifiable) criterion. The implementation fixes the root cause by reclassifying window-absent bindings from 'dangling' to the informational 'absent_in_window_unprobed' bucket, but there is no codebase evidence of per-item triage of any drift entry. The code change proves the reported dangling items were false positives (alive aged-out bindings misclassified); no per-item confirmed-404 GC, archive/delete propagation, or adoption action is present in the repository. The ticket comment from Joe Oakhart is an attestation, but since the criterion is not tagged [operator-attested], it cannot satisfy a codebase-verifiable criterion.
findings.remediation: Tag the triage criterion [operator-attested] and record a concrete attestation (e.g., referencing the run URL/id, observed drift counts = 0 for each alerting class, and timestamp). Alternatively, demonstrate in the codebase that each individual drift class's items have been resolved (confirmed-404 GC, archive/delete propagation, etc.).

citations.description: _empty_binding_drift() shows the section has 6 keys including 'dangling' and 'absent_in_window_unprobed'; the section is never empty in the JSON output.

The canary computes drift_total from ('would_terminal','dangling','local_gone','unbound_jira','retired_overlap') — it closes the ticket when this total is 0, not when binding_drift is structurally empty.

Ticket comment from Joe Oakhart: 'the 176 previously-false dangling entries are now correctly in the non-alerting informational bucket absent_in_window_unprobed=176' — confirming binding_drift is not empty.

findings.detail: The code fix correctly removes the false-positive dangling entries by routing window-absent bindings (no confirmed 404) to a new informational bucket 'absent_in_window_unprobed'. However, the JSON binding_drift section in the output is not literally empty: it still contains the 'absent_in_window_unprobed' list (which will be populated with ~176 entries per the ticket's own attestation), as well as the 'dangling' list. The canary workflow auto-close fires when alerting drift is zero (not when binding_drift is literally empty), and the canary still includes 'dangling' in its total. The AC as stated ('empty binding_drift section') is demonstrably not met in the implementation.
findings.remediation: Either update the AC to reflect the actual resolved state ('rebar bridge-fsck reports zero alerting binding_drift entries'), or tag it [operator-attested] and record a concrete canary run URL showing drift_found=false with a timestamp.

data.remediation: How to resolve the unmet criteria: for each one, decide whether the work is genuinely incomplete or simply undocumented. If a requirement is already satisfied but the proof is not discoverable from the code alone, add a comment to this ticket that documents the evidence — cite the concrete artifacts that meet the requirement (file paths and line ranges, commands and their output, links, or the reasoning that ties the work to the criterion). The completion verifier reads this ticket's comments, so evidence you record there is taken into account on the next verification. For any criterion whose work is genuinely unfinished, complete it, then re-verify.
